text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY / RADIATION ONCOLOGY
END-OF-TREATMENT NOTE — COMPLETION OF DEFINITIVE CHEMORADIATION

ATTENDING: Head and Neck Medical Oncology / Radiation Oncology (Co-signed)
VISIT TYPE: End-of-Treatment Assessment, In-Person

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Completion of definitive concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, base of tongue. End-of-treatment assessment and post-treatment planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 52-year-old Nigerian-American female, never-smoker, who presented initially with a 6-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort, globus sensation, odynophagia, and referred left-sided otalgia. She was found to have a 2.2 cm left base of tongue mass with left level IIA cervical lymphadenopathy (largest node 2.7 cm with central necrosis on CT). Biopsy confirmed HPV-positive (p16+, HPV RNA ISH+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, staged as T2N1M0, Stage I per AJCC 8th edition (HPV-positive oropharynx classification). Comprehensive staging PET-CT demonstrated a hypermetabolic left base of tongue mass (SUVmax 14.2) and hypermetabolic left level IIA node (SUVmax 11.8) without evidence of distant metastatic disease. NGS of the primary tumor showed a PIK3CA E545K mutation, TRAF3 loss-of-function, and other co-mutations, with low tumor mutational burden (4 mt/Mb), MSS status, and PD-L1 CPS 18. Following discussion at multidisciplinary tumor board, the patient proceeded with definitive concurrent chemoradiation given her excellent performance status and the absence of eligible de-escalation trial enrollment (exclusion due to necrotic nodal disease).

The patient completed all 35 fractions of IMRT using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB-VMAT) technique today, marking the end of her 7-week treatment course. The treatment fields encompassed the left base of tongue primary and left level IIA node (high-risk PTV, 70 Gy/35 fractions at 2.0 Gy/fx), left neck levels II-IV and bilateral retropharyngeal nodes (intermediate-risk PTV, 63 Gy/35 fractions at 1.8 Gy/fx), and elective bilateral neck levels II-V (low-risk PTV, 56 Gy/35 fractions at 1.6 Gy/fx). Concurrent systemic therapy was administered as high-dose bolus cis 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 22, and 43. Due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine rise to 1.4 mg/dL from baseline 0.8 mg/dL) and grade 3 mucositis at the time of cycle 3 (week 6), the third Platinol dose was administered at a reduced dose of 75 mg/m2. In total, she received 2 full cycles and 1 dose-reduced cycle of Platinol.

The treatment course was complicated by several significant toxicities. During week 4, the patient developed severe grade 3 oral mucositis with confluent pseudomembranous ulceration of the oropharynx and base of tongue, resulting in complete inability to tolerate oral intake and pain rated 8/10. She required a 5-day hospital admission for IV hydration, IV morphine via patient-controlled analgesia, and escalation to full enteral nutrition via the PEG tube (Osmolite 1.5 at 65 mL/hr continuous overnight plus bolus feeds). Oral candidiasis was identified during that admission and treated with fluconazole. Additionally, she developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the left neck during week 5-6, and grade 2 nephrotoxicity during week 6 as noted above, requiring outpatient IV hydration and magnesium repletion (nadir serum Mg 1.3 mg/dL). She also experienced grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 xerostomia. Radiation was delivered without interruption throughout the entire 7-week course despite these toxicities, which is commendable. She is currently maintained on full PEG tube feeds.

Pre-treatment weight was 68 kg (BMI 24.1). She has experienced approximately 5 kg total weight loss over the treatment course (current weight 63 kg).

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Fatigue present, grade 2, somewhat improved compared to peak treatment period. Appetite remains poor but slightly better than last week. No fever or chills.
HEENT: Oropharyngeal pain improving from peak; still present and rated approximately 5/10 at rest, 7/10 with attempted swallowing of thin liquids. Mucositis now grade 2 based on this assessment, down from grade 3 at week 4 admission. Xerostomia present, grade 1, with the characteristic "thick saliva" complaint that patients describe as frustrating. Left-sided otalgia has resolved. Odynophagia persists but is improving. No new neck swelling or mass sensation.
Pulmonary: No cough, no dyspnea, no hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Tolerating PEG tube feeds without nausea or tube-site discomfort. No abdominal pain, no diarrhea. Has been attempting small sips of thin liquid by mouth with limited success; minimal oral intake at this time.
Genitourinary: Urine output adequate. No dysuria. Recent creatinine elevation (1.4 mg/dL) being managed with IV hydration.
Musculoskeletal: Left neck skin tightening and stiffness beginning to be noticed. No bone pain.
Neurological: No neuropathy symptoms. No hearing changes on self-assessment.
Dermatological: Grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the left neck with moist desquamation in a small area of the lower left neck; otherwise dry desquamation.
Psychosocial: Patient reports feeling relieved to have completed treatment. She acknowledges anxiety about the upcoming response assessment scan. She is being supported by family. Social work has been involved.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: Temperature 36.8°C, Blood Pressure 118/74 mmHg, Heart Rate 82 bpm, Respiratory Rate 16 bpm, O2 Saturation 98% on room air. Weight: 63 kg (baseline 68 kg, loss of 5 kg, 7.4% from pre-treatment baseline).

General: The patient appears mildly fatigued but is alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. She is communicative and engaged. ECOG performance status assessed as 2 given ongoing significant treatment-related toxicity limiting some self-care activities, though she is ambulatory and not bed-bound.

Head and Neck:
- Scalp: No alopecia.
- Oral cavity and oropharynx: Mucous membranes erythematous with patchy pseudomembranous ulceration at the soft palate, posterior oropharyngeal wall, and base of tongue region; consistent with resolving grade 2 mucositis (improved from grade 3 on admission in week 4). No frank hemorrhagic ulceration. No evidence of oral candidiasis on today's examination. Salivary pooling of thick, ropy secretions. Tongue intact; limited extension due to discomfort but no fixed mass palpable on tongue base.
- Laryngoscopy: Flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy deferred today given ongoing mucositis and patient discomfort; will be performed at the 10-week post-treatment follow-up once mucositis has resolved and tissue is more evaluable.
- Neck: No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy in levels I, II, III, IV, or V bilaterally. The previously palpable firm 2.5 cm left level II lymph node is no longer identifiable on palpation. The left neck skin shows a zone of moist desquamation approximately 3 x 4 cm in the mid-left neck, consistent with grade 2 radiation dermatitis. The remainder of the left neck skin shows dry desquamation with erythema. There is mild soft tissue edema of the left anterior neck/submandibular region.
- Salivary glands: Left parotid region mildly tender to palpation. No discrete mass.
- Ears: External canals clear bilaterally. TMs visualized and intact.
- Thyroid: Not enlarged, no palpable nodule.

Cardiopulmonary: Regular rate and rhythm. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing or crackles.

Abdomen: PEG tube site on the left upper abdomen is clean, dry, and intact with no surrounding erythema, induration, or drainage. Site appropriate appearance for age of placement. Abdomen otherwise soft, non-tender.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact on examination.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:

(Most recent labs, obtained approximately 3 days prior to this visit)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 4.1 K/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (mild anemia, consistent with treatment-related effect)
- Platelets: 198 K/uL (normal)
- Absolute neutrophil count: 2.8 K/uL (adequate; no neutropenia)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 3.7 mEq/L
- Chloride: 101 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 16 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 1.4 mg/dL (elevated above baseline of 0.8 mg/dL; consistent with Platinol-related nephrotoxicity grade 2; being monitored — IV hydration administered on outpatient infusion basis this week)
- eGFR: 46 mL/min/1.73m2 (from 75+ at baseline)
- Glucose: 94 mg/dL
- Total protein: 6.1 g/dL
- Albumin: 3.2 g/dL (mildly low, consistent with poor nutritional intake and acute phase response during treatment)
- Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- ALT/AST: within normal limits
- Alkaline phosphatase: 78 U/L

Electrolytes/Other:
- Magnesium: 1.7 mg/dL (previously low at 1.3 mg/dL during week 6; now repleted, trending upward; patient on oral Mg supplementation 400 mg twice daily and received IV Mg repletion this week)
- Phosphorus: 3.1 mg/dL
- TSH: 1.6 (stable; patient on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily for baseline hypothyroidism)

Nutritional labs:
- Pre-albumin: 14 mg/dL (mildly depressed; nutritional support ongoing)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

No new imaging obtained at this visit. The treating team reviewed the pre-treatment baseline PET-CT which showed the left base of tongue primary (SUVmax 14.2) and left level IIA lymph node with central necrosis (SUVmax 11.8). Response assessment imaging will be formally obtained approximately 8-12 weeks post-completion of radiation to allow adequate time for resolution of radiation-related inflammation, which can cause false-positive FDG uptake and significantly confound interpretation. Post-treatment response assessment PET-CT has been ordered and will be scheduled for approximately 10 weeks from today. Clinical examination today is notable for the absence of palpable cervical lymphadenopathy, which is an encouraging early clinical finding, though definitive response assessment requires post-treatment imaging.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, base of tongue, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition Stage I), treatment completed:
   - The patient has successfully completed all 35 fractions of definitive IMRT with concurrent cis. She received 2 full cycles of cis 100 mg/m2 and 1 dose-reduced cycle (75 mg/m2) for a total cumulative dose of approximately 275 mg/m2 (normalized to her BSA). While dose reduction of the third cycle is not ideal, the clinical consensus in the literature is that delivery of ≥2 full cycles of high-dose cis with definitive radiotherapy provides adequate radiosensitization; she should be considered to have received adequate concurrent systemic therapy.
   - Clinical exam at end of treatment is encouraging: no palpable residual cervical lymphadenopathy (previously a firm, palpable left level II node). The primary site cannot be fully assessed clinically during the active mucositis period. Response assessment PET-CT ordered for approximately 10 weeks post-treatment; the patient will be contacted with the specific appointment. She was counseled that this 8-12 week waiting period is standard and necessary, and not a cause for alarm.
   - The patient was counseled regarding her prognosis: HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma treated with definitive chemoradiation carries an estimated 3-year overall survival of approximately 85-90% and 5-year disease-specific survival exceeding 85% in the never-smoking, complete responder population. She was told that the absence of palpable lymphadenopathy at end of treatment is a favorable sign.

2. Grade 2 oral mucositis (improving from grade 3):
   - Current regimen: Magic mouthwash (diphenhydramine/viscous lidocaine/Maalox) PRN, topical triamcinolone paste to accessible ulcerations, scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg q6h with breakthrough oxycodone 5-10 mg q4-6h PRN (transitioned from IV morphine PCA since hospital discharge). Patient using this regimen appropriately.
   - Will continue current analgesic regimen with plan to wean scheduled acetaminophen and decrease opioid use as mucositis resolves over the coming 3-4 weeks. Patient educated that mucosal healing following radiation typically requires 4-8 weeks.
   - Continue saline and sodium bicarbonate rinses ("salt and soda" solution) 4-5x daily for oral hygiene.
   - Close nursing phone follow-up twice weekly to monitor pain levels and analgesic requirements.

3. Nutritional status and PEG tube management:
   - Currently on full enteral nutrition via PEG (Osmolite 1.5, targeting 1800 kcal/day and 80g protein/day based on current weight and activity). Nutrition service to continue monitoring.
   - Pre-albumin 14 mg/dL indicates suboptimal but improving nutritional status. Will recheck in 2 weeks.
   - Will begin supervised oral trials in collaboration with speech-language pathology as mucositis resolves. Expectation is gradual oral intake resumption over 4-8 weeks post-treatment. PEG tube maintained until patient achieves >75% of caloric needs orally; removal deferred accordingly.
   - Patient educated that weight recovery and dysphagia rehabilitation are expected to take several months and that early SLP involvement is important.

4. Platinol-related nephrotoxicity (grade 2, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL from baseline 0.8 mg/dL):
   - Currently receiving additional IV hydration via outpatient infusion (1 L NS with MgSO4 supplementation today, twice this week). Will schedule 2 additional IV hydration sessions next week.
   - Avoid all nephrotoxic agents (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast agents if possible) until renal recovery.
   - Renal function labs (BMP, Mg, phosphorus) to be rechecked in 1 week. Anticipated trajectory: gradual recovery over 4-6 weeks with adequate hydration. If creatinine does not improve or worsens, nephrology consultation will be obtained.
   - No further cis to be administered (treatment course complete).

5. Magnesium wasting / hypomagnesemia:
   - Mg now 1.7 mg/dL (improved from nadir 1.3 mg/dL with IV and oral supplementation).
   - Continue oral magnesium oxide 400 mg twice daily. Supplement with IV MgSO4 during outpatient hydration sessions.
   - Recheck Mg with next BMP in 1 week. Target Mg > 1.8 mg/dL.
   - Hypomagnesemia may persist for months following Platinol due to renal tubular damage. Patient counseled regarding this and started on a magnesium-rich dietary supplement plan to be coordinated with nutritionist.

6. Grade 2 radiation dermatitis, left neck:
   - Area of moist desquamation identified in mid-left neck (~3 x 4 cm). Management: twice-daily application of moisture-retaining dressings (Mepilex Lite or equivalent non-adherent foam). Avoid products containing zinc oxide or alcohol. Continue gentle cleansing with mild soap.
   - Avoid shaving over affected area. Patient educated to keep area from sun exposure.
   - Anticipated healing over 3-4 weeks post-treatment completion. If signs of infection develop (increased pain, purulence, fever), she should contact the clinic immediately.

7. Fatigue (grade 2):
   - Counseled regarding expected post-treatment fatigue trajectory: typically peaks at end of treatment and improves over 4-8 weeks, though some residual fatigue may persist 3-6 months.
   - Encourage light walking and graduated activity as tolerated. Avoid excessive bed rest.
   - Mild anemia (Hgb 10.8 g/dL) contributing; no transfusion indicated at this level. Will monitor CBC at next visit.
   - Adequate sleep hygiene and psychosocial support emphasized.

8. Baseline hypothyroidism:
   - TSH 1.6 on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; within normal range at this time. Continue current dose. Will monitor TSH at 3-month follow-up; radiation to the neck can cause delayed radiation-induced hypothyroidism (typically onset 6-18 months post-treatment), and TSH monitoring will be maintained on a quarterly basis going forward.

9. GERD:
   - Patient reports GERD symptoms have been adequately managed during treatment. Continue omeprazole 20 mg daily (already taking). The mucositis and decreased oral intake during treatment may have actually reduced GERD symptoms temporarily; will reassess as oral intake resumes.

10. Psychosocial support and survivorship:
    - Social work follow-up to be scheduled. Patient expressed anxiety regarding response assessment scan and transitioning from active treatment to surveillance. Discussed that this is extremely common ("scanxiety") and offered referral to the head and neck cancer support group as well as individual counseling.
    - Patient to receive formal survivorship care plan at the 10-week post-treatment visit (coinciding with response assessment results).
    - Dental oncology follow-up to continue on a scheduled basis for fluoride treatments and monitoring of radiation caries risk.

FOLLOW-UP:
- Nursing/phone check-in: twice weekly for the next 2 weeks (monitoring mucositis resolution, pain levels, nutritional intake, tube feeds, and renal function lab results).
- Outpatient IV hydration: 2 additional sessions next week.
- Lab recheck (BMP, Mg, CBC, pre-albumin): 1 week from today.
- Speech-language pathology: continue weekly outpatient sessions; begin gradual oral trials when mucositis resolves to grade 1 or better.
- Radiation oncology follow-up: 2 weeks for wound check and skin assessment.
- Head and neck oncology / medical oncology: 10-week post-treatment visit with response assessment PET-CT results (already ordered; scheduling in progress).

The patient tolerated this lengthy discussion well and verbalized understanding of all instructions. Questions were answered. She was thanked for her strength and perseverance through a very demanding 7-week treatment course and reassured that completing all planned fractions without radiation break is an important achievement.

Electronic attestation by co-signing attending physicians (Medical Oncology and Radiation Oncology).
